justification: All 6 core rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus BR-7 as a policy complement). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Double-booking (BR-2) correctly invariant: true. Cancellation rules (BR-3, BR-4) appropriately marked invariant: false with clear justification distinguishing policy from data-integrity constraints. Role-based cancellation (patient vs. staff admin) correctly captured in BR-3/BR-4. 11 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, boundary conditions, and record access scoping.

observations.text: Exceptional invariant classification with detailed justifications explaining WHY each rule is or isn't an invariant — goes beyond simple true/false labeling

11 acceptance criteria covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1), double-booking (AC-2, AC-3), late cancellation (AC-5), unauthorized record access (AC-7), plus boundary conditions (AC-11)

BR-3 and BR-4 correctly split the cancellation rule into time-window policy (invariant: false) and role boundary (invariant: false with clear reasoning that it's authorization policy, not data integrity)
Edge cases are thorough and operationally realistic — race condition on double-booking, license revocation mid-lifecycle, note edit spanning the lock boundary
Confidence level 72 with well-articulated assumptions about boundary inclusivity, per-appointment vs per-relationship scoping, and license revocation gaps
Escalations identify three genuine ambiguities with concrete options and recommendations — demonstrates domain reasoning beyond the spec

BR-4 (admin-only cancellation after window) marked invariant: false is debatable — the role boundary between patient and admin is arguably a security/compliance invariant, though the justification for it being policy is reasonable
AC-3 (adjacent time slots) is an excellent edge case that tests the boundary of the double-booking rule — shows deep understanding of interval comparison logic

trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7, with BR-8 as a bonus distinguishing the 48h duration as configurable). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, record lock BR-7) correctly marked invariant: true. Cancellation rules (BR-3/BR-4) correctly split by role with the time window marked invariant: false and the role boundary captured. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: booking with licensed physician (AC-1), double-booking attempt (AC-2/AC-3), late cancellation attempt (AC-4/AC-5/AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-8/AC-9). Confidence level of 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism and temporal scope of treating physician access.

observations.text: Exceptional invariant justifications — each rule includes a clear rationale for why it is or is not an invariant, grounded in regulatory vs operational distinction
Correctly split cancellation into two rules (BR-3 patient time window, BR-4 admin override) capturing the role boundary explicitly
Bonus rule BR-8 demonstrates sophisticated analysis by separating the 48h duration (configurable policy) from the lock mechanism itself (invariant)
12 acceptance criteria with precise time-based examples, including the edge-exclusive boundary case (AC-3) which shows deep scheduling domain understanding
Rich edge cases section identifying 8 scenarios including concurrency, license revocation, and temporal access scope — all operationally realistic
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, particularly the treating physician temporal scope ambiguity
Confidence level of 72 is appropriately calibrated — high enough to reflect clear inputs but with explicit low-confidence areas and assumptions noted

trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6) with correct invariant classifications. Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note locking BR-6) all marked invariant: true. Added a 7th rule (BR-7, note lock irreversibility) which strengthens coverage. Cancellation rules correctly split into patient (BR-3, invariant: false for the time window) and admin (BR-4, invariant: false), capturing the role boundary. 14 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the minimum of 4. Confidence level of 72 is within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, boundary conditions, and record scoping.

observations.text: Correctly classified all regulatory rules (license, visibility, record lock) as invariant: true while marking the cancellation time window and admin override as invariant: false — showing clear distinction between regulatory constraints and operational policies
14 acceptance criteria with excellent boundary testing (AC-3 adjacent slots, AC-6 exact 2-hour boundary, AC-11/AC-12 48-hour boundary) go well beyond the minimum requirement
Rich edge cases section identifying 9 scenarios including concurrency concerns, license revocation, and multi-physician record scoping — demonstrates deep domain thinking
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, particularly the physician license revocation gap and record access scoping ambiguity
Confidence level of 72 with 5 specific low-confidence areas and 6 explicit assumptions shows appropriate epistemic humility without being underconfident
Ubiquitous language section is thorough with 12 well-defined terms, establishing clear domain vocabulary

Bounded context identified as single 'Appointment & Consultation Management' rather than separating 'appointment scheduling' and 'medical records access' as two distinct bounded contexts per the expected behavior
